responses_by_run.answer: Athena turned Arachne into a spider as punishment for her pride and defiance [1]. Arachne was a mortal weaver of extraordinary skill who refused to acknowledge Athena's superiority in the art of weaving [1]. When Athena, disguised as an old woman, advised Arachne to pray for the goddess's pardon, Arachne scornfully rejected her and challenged the goddess to a weaving contest [1]. After Athena won the contest, she was enraged by Arachne's skill and defiance [2]. Athena destroyed Arachne's web and struck her repeatedly [2]. When Arachne attempted to hang herself, Athena saved her from death but transformed her into a spider, condemning her to weave forever as eternal punishment for her presumption in rivaling a goddess [3].

citations.author: Ovid
citations.stance: describes Arachne's challenge and Athena's initial response

citations.author: Ovid
citations.stance: describes the weaving contest and Athena's rage

citations.author: Ovid
citations.stance: describes Arachne's transformation into a spider

responses_by_run.answer: Prometheus was punished for two interconnected transgressions. First, he stole fire and gave it to humans [1]. According to Apollodorus, Prometheus hid fire in a stalk of fennel and gave it to men, and when Zeus learned of this theft, he ordered Hephaestus to nail Prometheus to Mount Caucasus as punishment [1]. Second, Hesiod indicates that Prometheus was also punished for attempting to deceive Zeus by dividing sacrificial portions unfairly at Mecone, setting aside inferior portions for Zeus while hiding the better meat [2]. His punishment involved being bound with inextricable chains and having an eagle devour his liver daily, which would regenerate each night [3].

Odysseus escaped Polyphemus through a two-part plan. First, he blinded the Cyclops by sharpening a wooden club, heating it in fire, and driving it into Polyphemus's eye with the help of his comrades [1]. When Polyphemus cried out for help from other Cyclopes, they misunderstood his cries of "Nobody" and left him alone [2]. Second, after the blinding, Odysseus tied the sheep together in groups of three and hid himself beneath the largest ram by clinging to its fleece [3]. When Polyphemus opened the cave door in the morning to let his flocks out to pasture, he felt the sheep as they passed but could not detect the men hidden beneath them, allowing Odysseus and his surviving comrades to escape to their ship [4].

citations.author: Homer
citations.stance: describes the final escape to the ship

responses_by_run.answer: At the wedding of Peleus and Thetis, the gods celebrated the marriage with feast and song on Mount Pelion [1]. The marriage took place after Peleus seized Thetis despite her shape-shifting resistance, holding her fast as she transformed into fire, water, and various beasts until she resumed her original form [1]. As wedding gifts, Chiron gave Peleus an ashen spear [1], and Poseidon gave him immortal horses named Balius and Xanthus [1].

citations.author: Apollodorus
citations.stance: describes the wedding of Peleus and Thetis

responses_by_run.answer: Demeter stopped the crops from growing because she was grieving over the abduction of her daughter [1]. She vowed that she would never let fruit spring out of the ground until she beheld her own fair-faced daughter with her eyes [1]. She was so wrathful in her heart that no one of the gods, not even Zeus's messengers, could persuade her to relent [2].

citations.author: Anonymous ("Homeric")
citations.stance: describes Demeter's grief and vow regarding the crops

citations.author: Anonymous ("Homeric")
citations.stance: describes Demeter's wrath and resistance to persuasion

responses_by_run.answer: The Olympians who are children of Cronus include Zeus [1][2][3][4][5], Poseidon [1][2][3], Hera [1], and Demeter [1][2]. These six deities are consistently identified across multiple ancient sources as the offspring of Cronus, the Titan who ruled before the Olympian age.
responses_by_run.sqlGenerated:
SELECT child.name AS name,
       s.author AS author, s.work AS work, r.passage_ref AS passage_ref, s.stance AS stance
FROM relationships r
JOIN entities parent ON parent.id = r.from_id
JOIN entities child ON child.id = r.to_id
JOIN sources s ON s.id = r.source_id
WHERE r.relation = 'parent_of' AND parent.name ILIKE 'Cronus' AND child.type = 'olympian'

responses_by_run.answer: Several heroes are identified as children of Zeus in classical sources. Aeneas [1] is described as a son of Zeus, as is Arcesilaus [2]. Additionally, Iasion [3] is counted among Zeus's children.
responses_by_run.sqlGenerated:
SELECT child.name AS name,
       s.author AS author, s.work AS work, r.passage_ref AS passage_ref, s.stance AS stance
FROM relationships r
JOIN entities parent ON parent.id = r.from_id
JOIN entities child ON child.id = r.to_id
JOIN sources s ON s.id = r.source_id
WHERE r.relation = 'parent_of' AND parent.name ILIKE 'Zeus' AND child.type = 'hero'
ORDER BY child.name ASC

responses_by_run.answer: Perseus encountered several monsters and adversaries throughout his adventures. Most notably, he slew the Gorgon Medusa [1], beheading her in a famous feat. He then used the Gorgon's head as a weapon against other foes: he turned Phineus and his fellow conspirators to stone by displaying the severed head [2], and similarly transformed Polydectes and his assembled friends to stone [3]. During the battle at Seriphus, Perseus encountered and slew numerous human opponents including Erithus (son of Actor), Caucasian Abaris, Polydaemon, Sperchius, Lycetus, Elyces, Clytus, Phlegias, Molpeus, and Ethemon [4][5]. Additionally, Perseus encountered the Phorcides—three old women sisters of the Gorgons named Enyo, Pephredo, and Dino [1]—though rather than slaying them, he negotiated with them for information [1].

Zeus's lineage traces back through several divine generations to Chaos itself. According to Hesiod's cosmological account, Earth (Gaia) emerged as a primordial deity [1], and from Earth came Cronus [2], who became Zeus's father. Cronus is attested across multiple sources: Hesiod presents him in his cosmological framework [2], Apollodorus includes him in his mythographic handbook [3], and Homer references him in both the Iliad and Odyssey as a figure of divine authority [4][5]. The Homeric Hymns also acknowledge Cronus's significance in the divine genealogy [6]. Thus, the lineage flows from the primordial Chaos through Earth and Cronus to Zeus, establishing the Titan generation as the crucial link between the oldest cosmic forces and the Olympian order.
responses_by_run.sqlGenerated:
WITH RECURSIVE lineage AS (
    SELECT e.id, e.name, NULL::text AS source_id, NULL::text AS passage_ref,
           1 AS depth, ARRAY[e.id] AS visited
    FROM entities e
    WHERE e.name ILIKE 'Zeus'

    UNION ALL

    SELECT parent.id, parent.name, r.source_id, r.passage_ref,
           lineage.depth + 1, lineage.visited || parent.id
    FROM lineage
    JOIN relationships r ON r.to_id = lineage.id AND r.relation = 'parent_of'
    JOIN entities parent ON parent.id = r.from_id
    WHERE NOT parent.id = ANY(lineage.visited) AND lineage.depth < 20
)
SELECT lineage.name AS name,
       s.author AS author, s.work AS work, lineage.passage_ref AS passage_ref, s.stance AS stance
FROM lineage
LEFT JOIN sources s ON s.id = lineage.source_id
ORDER BY lineage.depth ASC, lineage.name ASC

responses_by_run.sqlGenerated:
WITH troy_deaths AS (
    SELECT DISTINCT vc.subject_entity_id
    FROM variant_claims vc
    WHERE vc.claim_type = 'death' AND vc.claim_value ILIKE '%Troy%'
)
SELECT DISTINCT h.name AS hero_name,
       parent.name AS divine_parent,
       s.author AS author, s.work AS work, r.passage_ref AS passage_ref, s.stance AS stance
FROM entities h
JOIN relationships r ON r.to_id = h.id AND r.relation = 'parent_of'
JOIN entities parent ON parent.id = r.from_id
JOIN sources s ON s.id = r.source_id
JOIN troy_deaths td ON td.subject_entity_id = h.id
WHERE h.type = 'hero'
  AND parent.type IN ('primordial', 'titan', 'olympian', 'other_god')
ORDER BY h.name ASC, parent.name ASC

responses_by_run.answer: Achilles' divine lineage to Zeus traces through both his paternal and maternal lines. On his father's side, Peleus was the son of Aeacus, who was himself the son of Zeus and the mortal woman Aegina [1]. Through this genealogy, Achilles was the grandson of Zeus [1]. On his mother's side, Achilles' mother Thetis was a goddess—specifically a Nereid, daughter of Nereus [1]—making Achilles uniquely descended from both Zeus and a divine mother. Ovid emphasizes the distinction of this dual divine heritage, noting that while other men might be grandsons of Zeus, Peleus alone was honored with a goddess as his bride, a distinction that elevated Achilles' status among mortals [2][3].
responses_by_run.sqlGenerated:
WITH RECURSIVE lineage AS (
    SELECT e.id, e.name, e.type, NULL::text AS source_id, NULL::text AS passage_ref,
           1 AS depth, ARRAY[e.id] AS visited
    FROM entities e
    WHERE e.name ILIKE 'Achilles'

    UNION ALL

    SELECT parent.id, parent.name, parent.type, r.source_id, r.passage_ref,
           lineage.depth + 1, lineage.visited || parent.id
    FROM lineage
    JOIN relationships r ON r.to_id = lineage.id AND r.relation = 'parent_of'
    JOIN entities parent ON parent.id = r.from_id
    WHERE NOT parent.id = ANY(lineage.visited) AND lineage.depth < 20
)
SELECT lineage.depth AS generation_level,
       lineage.name AS entity_name,
       lineage.type AS entity_type,
       s.author AS author,
       s.work AS work,
       s.stance AS stance,
       lineage.passage_ref AS passage_ref
FROM lineage
JOIN sources s ON s.id = lineage.source_id
WHERE lineage.source_id IS NOT NULL
ORDER BY lineage.depth ASC

responses_by_run.answer: Aphrodite's parentage is described differently across ancient sources. According to Homer, she was the daughter of Dione [1][2], while Apollodorus also identifies Dione as her mother and Zeus as her father [3]. However, other sources present an alternative account: Hesiod describes her as born from the sea foam formed by the severed members of Heaven, cast into the sea by Cronus [4]. Additionally, several sources identify Zeus as her father, including the Homeric Hymns [5], Homer's own accounts in the Iliad and Odyssey [6][7][8], and Ovid in the Metamorphoses [9].

responses_by_run.answer: Io's father is identified differently across ancient sources. According to Apollodorus in the Bibliotheca, Io was the daughter of Iasus in the main narrative [1], though Apollodorus also notes that Castor and many tragedians called her the daughter of Inachus [2], while Hesiod and Acusilaus made her the daughter of Piren [3]. Ovid, in the Metamorphoses, identifies Io as the daughter of Inachus [4].

responses_by_run.answer: Achilles died by being shot with an arrow. According to Ovid, Paris shot an arrow at Achilles that was guided by Apollo, at Apollo's and Neptune's instigation [1]. Apollodorus similarly records that Achilles would die by the hand of Apollo, which occurred after he killed Tenes despite a forewarning from his mother Thetis [2]. Homer's account adds that the dying Hector foretold Achilles would be slain by Paris and Phoebus Apollo at the Scaean gate [3], and that Thetis had previously foretold her son would perish beneath the wall of the Trojans by the swift missiles of Apollo [4]. Multiple sources confirm that both Paris and Apollo were instrumental in his death [5][6], with Achilles fated to meet his end far from his native land [7].

responses_by_run.answer: Achilles withdrew from the fighting after his quarrel with Agamemnon because Agamemnon took his prize of honor, Briseis. According to Homer's account, Achilles was angry that Agamemnon threatened to seize Briseis, the prize he had earned through his own efforts in battle, while Agamemnon's own prize remained far greater [1]. Achilles declared he would return home to Phthia rather than remain dishonored and continue fighting to enrich Agamemnon [1]. Apollodorus's account similarly states that Achilles did not go forth to war because he was angry on account of Briseis, the daughter of Chryses the priest [2].

Athena turned Arachne into a spider as punishment for her pride and defiance [1]. Arachne, a mortal weaver of extraordinary skill, refused to acknowledge Athena's superiority in the art of weaving [1]. When Athena appeared to her in disguise as an old woman and advised her to pray for the goddess's pardon, Arachne scornfully rejected the advice and challenged the goddess to a weaving contest [1]. After Arachne completed her web, Athena found it flawless but was enraged that Arachne had woven scenes depicting the gods' wicked actions [2]. Athena destroyed Arachne's web and struck her repeatedly with her shuttle [2]. Arachne then attempted to hang herself, but Athena saved her life while pronouncing a doom: she would dangle on a cord forever, and her punishment would extend to all her descendants [2]. Athena then sprinkled Arachne with magical herbs, causing her to shrink and transform into a spider, condemned to weave webs for eternity [3].

Prometheus was punished for two primary offenses. First, he stole fire and gave it to humans [1]. According to Apollodorus, Prometheus hid fire in a stalk of fennel and gave it to men, and when Zeus learned of this theft, he ordered Hephaestus to nail Prometheus to Mount Caucasus as punishment [1]. Second, Hesiod indicates that Prometheus was also punished for attempting to deceive Zeus by dividing portions of an ox unfairly during a dispute between gods and mortals at Mecone, setting aside inferior portions for Zeus while disguising them with fat [2]. His punishment involved being bound with chains and having an eagle devour his liver daily, which would regenerate each night [3].

responses_by_run.answer: Odysseus escaped Polyphemus through a two-part plan. First, he blinded the Cyclops by sharpening a club, heating it in fire, and driving it into Polyphemus's eye with the help of his comrades [1][2]. When Polyphemus cried out for help from other Cyclopes, they misunderstood his cries of "Nobody" and left him alone [2]. Second, after the blinding, Odysseus tied the sheep together in groups of three and had his men cling to the middle sheep while he himself clung to the largest ram [1][2]. When Polyphemus opened the cave door to let his flocks out to pasture, he felt the sheep as they passed but could not catch the men hidden beneath them [1][2]. Odysseus and his comrades thus escaped with the sheep to their ship [1][2].

At the wedding of Peleus and Thetis, the gods celebrated the marriage with feast and song on Mount Pelion [1]. The marriage took place after Peleus seized Thetis despite her shape-shifting resistance, holding her fast as she transformed into fire, water, and various beasts until she resumed her original form [1]. As wedding gifts, Chiron gave Peleus an ashen spear [1], and Poseidon gave him immortal horses named Balius and Xanthus [1].

Demeter stopped the crops from growing because she was grief-stricken and angry over the abduction of her daughter [1]. She vowed that she would never let fruit spring out of the ground until she beheld her own fair-faced daughter with her eyes [2].

The Olympians who are children of Cronus include Zeus [1][2][3][4][5], Poseidon [1][2][3], Hera [1], and Demeter [1][2]. These deities are consistently identified across multiple ancient sources as the offspring of the Titan Cronus.

responses_by_run.answer: Perseus encountered several monsters during his adventures. Most notably, he slew the Gorgon Medusa [1][2], beheading her in a famous feat. Beyond Medusa, Perseus used the severed Gorgon's head as a weapon against other adversaries. He turned Phineus and his fellow conspirators to stone by displaying the Gorgon's head [2][3]. Similarly, he transformed Polydectes and his gathered friends into stone using the same gruesome weapon [4][5]. While Perseus also encountered and slew various human warriors—including Erithus, Caucasian Abaris, Polydaemon, Sperchius, Lycetus, Elyces, Clytus, and Phlegias—during the battle at Seriphus [3][4][5], these were men rather than monsters in the proper sense.

Zeus's lineage traces back through several divine generations to primordial Chaos. According to Hesiod's cosmological account, Earth (Gaia) emerged as a fundamental force in the cosmos [1]. From Earth came Cronus [2], who became the leader of the Titans. Cronus is attested across multiple sources: Hesiod describes him in his cosmological framework [3], while Homer references him in his poetic narratives [4][5], and the Homeric Hymns also preserve his role in the mythological tradition [6]. Apollodorus's mythographic handbook similarly documents Cronus as a key figure in divine genealogy [7]. Zeus, as the son of Cronus, thus descends from Earth and the primordial divine order that emerged from Chaos in Greek mythological tradition.

responses_by_run.answer: The provided sources do not contain information about which heroes had divine parents and died at Troy.
responses_by_run.sqlGenerated:
WITH heroes_with_divine_parents AS (
    SELECT DISTINCT child.id, child.name
    FROM relationships r
    JOIN entities parent ON parent.id = r.from_id
    JOIN entities child ON child.id = r.to_id
    WHERE r.relation = 'parent_of'
      AND parent.type IN ('primordial', 'titan', 'olympian', 'other_god')
      AND child.type = 'hero'
)
SELECT DISTINCT h.name AS name,
       s.author AS author, s.work AS work, r.passage_ref AS passage_ref, s.stance AS stance
FROM heroes_with_divine_parents h
JOIN relationships r ON r.from_id = h.id AND r.relation = 'killed_by'
JOIN entities killer ON killer.id = r.to_id
JOIN sources s ON s.id = r.source_id
WHERE r.passage_ref ILIKE '%Troy%'
   OR EXISTS (
       SELECT 1 FROM myths m
       JOIN myth_participants mp ON mp.myth_id = m.id
       WHERE mp.entity_id = h.id AND m.title ILIKE '%Troy%'
   )
ORDER BY h.name ASC

responses_by_run.answer: Achilles' divine lineage connects to Zeus through multiple pathways. On his paternal side, Achilles descended from Zeus through his grandfather Aeacus, who was the son of Zeus and the mortal woman Aegina [1]. Aeacus fathered Peleus, Achilles' father [1]. On his maternal side, Achilles' mother was Thetis, a Nereid or sea-goddess [2]. According to the sources, Peleus seized Thetis in her cave, and though she initially resisted by changing her shape, she eventually acknowledged that 'some divinity has given you the power' and revealed herself as Thetis [3]. Through this union, Thetis became the mother of Achilles [1]. Thus Achilles was of mixed divine and mortal descent on both sides of his family—descended from Zeus through his paternal grandfather Aeacus, while also being the son of a goddess through his mother Thetis [1].

responses_by_run.answer: Aphrodite's parentage is described differently across ancient sources. According to Homer in the Iliad, Aphrodite was the daughter of Dione [1], a tradition also recorded by Apollodorus in the Bibliotheca [2]. However, other sources attribute her to a different parentage: Homer himself elsewhere in the Iliad, Odyssey, and other works identifies her as the daughter of Zeus [3][4][5], a version also found in the Homeric Hymns [6] and later in Ovid's Metamorphoses [7]. Hesiod's Theogony presents yet another account, describing Aphrodite as born from the sea foam formed by the severed members of Heaven, cast into the sea by Cronus [8].

JOIN sources s ON s.id = r.source_id
WHERE r.relation = 'parent_of' AND child.name ILIKE 'Aphrodite'
ORDER BY parent.name ASC

responses_by_run.answer: Io's father is identified differently across ancient sources. According to Apollodorus in the Bibliotheca, the main narrative presents Io as the daughter of Iasus [1], though Apollodorus also notes that Castor and many tragedians call her the daughter of Inachus [2], and that Hesiod and Acusilaus identify her father as Piren [3]. Ovid, in the Metamorphoses, consistently identifies Io as the daughter of Inachus [4].

responses_by_run.answer: Achilles died by being struck with an arrow. According to Ovid, Paris shot an arrow that was guided by Apollo to fatally strike Achilles while he fought the Trojan warriors, with both Apollo and Neptune instigating the attack [1]. Apollodorus records that Thetis had forewarned Achilles he would die by the hand of Apollo if he killed Tenes, which he did [2]. Homer's account adds that the dying Hector foretold Achilles would be slain by Paris and Phoebus Apollo at the Scaean gate [3], and that Thetis had warned her son he would perish beneath the Trojan wall by Apollo's swift missiles [4]. After his death, Achilles was buried with Patroclus in the White Isle [5].

responses_by_run.answer: Achilles withdrew from the fighting after his quarrel with Agamemnon because Agamemnon took his war prize, Briseis. According to Homer, Achilles was angry that Agamemnon threatened to seize his prize despite Achilles bearing the brunt of the fierce battle, and he declared he would return home to Phthia rather than remain dishonored and pile up riches for Agamemnon [1]. Apollodorus similarly reports that Achilles did not go forth to war because he was angry on account of Briseis, the daughter of Chryses the priest [2].

citations.stance: Achilles withdrew because Agamemnon took Briseis as his prize, and Achilles was angered at being dishonored

responses_by_run.answer: Athena turned Arachne into a spider as punishment for her pride and defiance [1]. Arachne was an exceptionally skilled weaver who refused to acknowledge Athena's superiority in the art of weaving [1]. When Athena appeared to her in disguise as an old woman and advised her to pray for the goddess's pardon, Arachne scornfully rejected the advice and challenged the goddess to a weaving contest [1]. After Athena examined Arachne's woven work in the contest and found it flawless, she became enraged that a mortal had such skill and dared to rival her [2]. Athena destroyed Arachne's web and struck her repeatedly with her shuttle [2]. In despair, Arachne attempted to hang herself, but Athena saved her life while pronouncing a curse: she would dangle on a cord forever as punishment, and her descendants would do the same [2]. Athena then sprinkled her with magical herbs, causing her body to shrink and transform into a spider, condemning her to weave webs for eternity [3].

Prometheus was punished for two primary offenses. First, he stole fire and gave it to humans [1]. According to Apollodorus, Prometheus hid fire in a stalk of fennel and gave it to men, and when Zeus learned of this theft, he ordered Hephaestus to nail Prometheus to Mount Caucasus as punishment [1]. Second, Hesiod indicates that Prometheus was also punished for attempting to deceive Zeus by dividing sacrificial portions unfairly at Mecone, setting aside bones covered in fat for Zeus while giving the flesh to mortals [2]. His punishment involved being bound with inextricable chains and having an eagle devour his liver daily, which would regenerate each night [3].

citations.author: Apollodorus
citations.stance: Prometheus stole fire in a fennel stalk and gave it to men; Zeus ordered Hephaestus to nail him to Mount Caucasus

citations.author: Hesiod
citations.stance: Prometheus deceived Zeus by dividing sacrificial portions unfairly at Mecone, giving bones covered in fat to Zeus and flesh to mortals

citations.author: Hesiod
citations.stance: Prometheus was bound with inextricable chains and had an eagle devour his liver daily, which regenerated each night

responses_by_run.answer: Odysseus escaped Polyphemus through a two-part plan. First, he blinded the Cyclops by sharpening a wooden club, heating it in fire, and driving it into Polyphemus's eye with the help of his comrades [1][2]. When Polyphemus cried out for help from other Cyclopes, they misunderstood his cries of "Nobody" and left him alone [1][2]. Second, after the blinding, Odysseus tied the sheep together in groups of three and hid himself under the largest ram, clinging to its fleece [1][2]. When Polyphemus opened the cave door to let his flocks out to pasture, he felt the sheep as they passed but could not detect the men hidden beneath them, allowing Odysseus and his surviving comrades to escape to their ship [1][2].

citations.author: Homer
citations.stance: describes Odysseus's blinding of Polyphemus and the deception with 'Nobody'

citations.author: Homer
citations.stance: describes Odysseus hiding under the ram and escaping with his men

responses_by_run.answer: At the wedding of Peleus and Thetis, the gods celebrated the marriage with feast and song on Mount Pelion [1]. Chiron gave Peleus an ashen spear as a wedding gift, and Poseidon gave him immortal horses named Balius and Xanthus [1]. The marriage took place after Peleus seized Thetis despite her shape-shifting resistance, holding her fast as she transformed into fire, water, and beasts until she resumed her original form [1].

Demeter stopped the crops from growing because she was grieving over the abduction of her daughter [1]. She vowed that she would never let fruit spring out of the ground until she beheld her own fair-faced daughter with her eyes [2].

citations.author: Anonymous ("Homeric")
citations.stance: describes Demeter's grief over her daughter's abduction

citations.author: Anonymous ("Homeric")
citations.stance: describes Demeter's vow to withhold crops until seeing her daughter

responses_by_run.answer: The Olympians who are children of Cronus include Zeus [1][2][3][4][5], Poseidon [1][2][3], Hera [1], and Demeter [1][2]. These deities are consistently identified across multiple ancient sources as the offspring of Cronus, the Titan who ruled before the Olympian age.
responses_by_run.sqlGenerated:
SELECT child.name AS name,
       s.author AS author, s.work AS work, r.passage_ref AS passage_ref, s.stance AS stance
FROM relationships r
JOIN entities parent ON parent.id = r.from_id
JOIN entities child ON child.id = r.to_id
JOIN sources s ON s.id = r.source_id
WHERE r.relation = 'parent_of' AND parent.name ILIKE 'Cronus' AND child.type = 'olympian'

responses_by_run.answer: Several heroes are identified as children of Zeus in classical sources. Aeneas [1] is described as a son of Zeus, as is Arcesilaus [2]. Additionally, Iasion [3] is counted among Zeus's children.
responses_by_run.sqlGenerated:
SELECT child.name AS name,
       s.author AS author, s.work AS work, r.passage_ref AS passage_ref, s.stance AS stance
FROM relationships r
JOIN entities parent ON parent.id = r.from_id
JOIN entities child ON child.id = r.to_id
JOIN sources s ON s.id = r.source_id
WHERE r.relation = 'parent_of' AND parent.name ILIKE 'Zeus' AND child.type = 'hero'
ORDER BY child.name ASC

responses_by_run.answer: Perseus encountered several monsters and adversaries throughout his adventures. Most notably, he slew the Gorgon Medusa [1], beheading her and obtaining her severed head as a powerful weapon. He later used this head against Phineus and his fellow conspirators, turning them to stone [2]. Similarly, Perseus displayed the Gorgon's head to Polydectes and his gathered friends, transforming them to stone as well [3]. During the battle at Seriphus, Perseus encountered and slew various human opponents including Erithus (son of Actor), Caucasian Abaris, Polydaemon, Sperchius, Lycetus, Elyces, Clytus, and Phlegias [4]. Additionally, along his journey to Medusa's dwelling, Perseus witnessed men and animals that had been turned to stone by Medusa's gaze, though these were victims rather than monsters he directly confronted [1].

Zeus's lineage traces back through several divine generations to Chaos itself. According to Hesiod's cosmological account, Earth (Gaia) emerged as a primordial deity [1], and from Earth came Cronus [2], who became Zeus's father. Cronus is attested across multiple sources: Hesiod presents him in his cosmological framework [2], Apollodorus includes him in his mythographic handbook [3], and Homer references him in his poetic accounts [4][5]. The genealogical chain thus extends from the primordial Chaos through Earth and Cronus to Zeus, establishing the Titan-Olympian succession that defines Greek mythological history.
responses_by_run.sqlGenerated:
WITH RECURSIVE lineage AS (
    SELECT e.id, e.name, NULL::text AS source_id, NULL::text AS passage_ref,
           1 AS depth, ARRAY[e.id] AS visited
    FROM entities e
    WHERE e.name ILIKE 'Zeus'

    UNION ALL

    SELECT parent.id, parent.name, r.source_id, r.passage_ref,
           lineage.depth + 1, lineage.visited || parent.id
    FROM lineage
    JOIN relationships r ON r.to_id = lineage.id AND r.relation = 'parent_of'
    JOIN entities parent ON parent.id = r.from_id
    WHERE NOT parent.id = ANY(lineage.visited) AND lineage.depth < 20
)
SELECT lineage.name AS name,
       s.author AS author, s.work AS work, lineage.passage_ref AS passage_ref, s.stance AS stance
FROM lineage
LEFT JOIN sources s ON s.id = lineage.source_id
ORDER BY lineage.depth ASC, lineage.name ASC

responses_by_run.answer: The provided material does not contain information about which heroes had a divine parent and died at Troy.
responses_by_run.sqlGenerated:
WITH troy_deaths AS (
    SELECT DISTINCT vc.subject_entity_id
    FROM variant_claims vc
    WHERE vc.claim_type = 'death' AND vc.claim_value ILIKE '%Troy%'
)
SELECT DISTINCT h.name AS hero_name,
       parent.name AS divine_parent,
       s.author AS author, s.work AS work, r.passage_ref AS passage_ref, s.stance AS stance
FROM entities h
JOIN relationships r ON r.to_id = h.id AND r.relation = 'parent_of'
JOIN entities parent ON parent.id = r.from_id
JOIN sources s ON s.id = r.source_id
JOIN troy_deaths td ON td.subject_entity_id = h.id
WHERE h.type = 'hero'
  AND parent.type IN ('primordial', 'titan', 'olympian', 'other_god')
ORDER BY h.name ASC, parent.name ASC

responses_by_run.answer: Achilles' divine lineage to Zeus traces through both his parents. His father Peleus was the son of Aeacus, who was himself the son of Zeus and the mortal woman Aegina [1]. This made Peleus the grandson of Zeus [2]. Peleus married Thetis, a Nereid and daughter of Nereus [1], who was a goddess rather than a mortal woman. Through this union, Achilles became the grandson of Zeus through his father's line [1] and the son of a goddess through his mother [1]. Peleus was thus distinguished among heroes for having divine ancestry on both sides of his parentage, being 'grandson of great Jove' (Zeus) and 'honored with a goddess for a wife' [2].
responses_by_run.sqlGenerated:
WITH RECURSIVE lineage AS (
    SELECT e.id, e.name, NULL::text AS source_id, NULL::text AS passage_ref,
           1 AS depth, ARRAY[e.id] AS visited
    FROM entities e
    WHERE e.name ILIKE 'Achilles'

    UNION ALL

    SELECT parent.id, parent.name, r.source_id, r.passage_ref,
           lineage.depth + 1, lineage.visited || parent.id
    FROM lineage
    JOIN relationships r ON r.to_id = lineage.id AND r.relation = 'parent_of'
    JOIN entities parent ON parent.id = r.from_id
    WHERE NOT parent.id = ANY(lineage.visited) AND lineage.depth < 20
)
SELECT lineage.depth AS generation_level,
       lineage.name AS entity_name,
       parent_e.type AS entity_type,
       s.author AS author,
       s.work AS work,
       lineage.passage_ref AS passage_ref,
       s.stance AS stance
FROM lineage
LEFT JOIN sources s ON s.id = lineage.source_id
LEFT JOIN entities parent_e ON parent_e.id = lineage.id
WHERE lineage.source_id IS NOT NULL OR lineage.depth = 1
ORDER BY lineage.depth ASC, lineage.name ASC

responses_by_run.answer: Aphrodite's parentage varies across ancient sources. According to Homer, she was the daughter of Dione [1][2], while Apollodorus also identifies Dione as her mother and Zeus as her father [3]. However, other sources present different accounts: Hesiod describes her as born from the sea foam formed by the severed members of Heaven, cast into the sea by Cronus [4]. Additionally, Homer in other works identifies Zeus as her father [5][6], as do the Homeric Hymns [7], and later authors including Ovid [8].

responses_by_run.answer:
Achilles died by being shot with an arrow. According to Homer, the dying Hector foretold that Achilles would be slain by Paris and Phoebus Apollo at the Scaean gate [1], and Thetis forewarned her son that he would perish beneath the wall of the Trojans by the swift missiles of Apollo [2]. Multiple sources confirm this fate: Homer also states that Achilles was fated to be slain in fight by a god and a mortal, far from his father and mother [3], while Apollodorus records that Thetis forewarned Achilles he would die by the hand of Apollo if he slew Tenes [4].

Regarding the specific circumstances of his death, Ovid describes how Apollo, at Neptune's instigation, guided Paris's arrow to strike Achilles fatally [5], though Ovid also notes in another passage that Achilles was killed by Paris and Apollo with arrows or shafts [6]. After his death, Achilles was buried with Patroclus in the White Isle [7], and Ajax carried his dead body from the battlefield [8].

responses_by_run.answer: Achilles withdrew from the fighting after his quarrel with Agamemnon because Agamemnon took his prize, Briseis. According to Homer's account, Achilles was angry that Agamemnon threatened to seize his prize despite Achilles having toiled greatly in battle to obtain it, while Agamemnon's own prize remained far greater [1]. Apollodorus provides a simpler account, stating that Achilles did not go forth to war because he was angry on account of Briseis, the daughter of Chryses the priest [2].
